message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 5 (2 todo)
Recent changes: No implementation change for test 5 — overpopulation already covered by the existing `survives` predicate.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Apply Simple Design Rules / APP; attempt a refactoring or document why none is warranted; keep all tests green.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 5 tests pass (2 todo). Now I'll work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Refactor Phase Analysis

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing each name against what the latest test (overpopulation) revealed:

- **`isNeighbor(a, b)`**: Returns true when two cells are adjacent (within 1 in both axes, excluding self). Name clearly reveals intent. ✅
- **`countLiveNeighbors(cell, cells)`**: Counts adjacent live cells. Clear and accurate. ✅
- **`survives(liveNeighbors)`**: Returns whether a live cell survives given its neighbor count. The overpopulation test (test 5) confirmed this predicate already handles the >3 case correctly (2 or 3 survives; 4+ dies). The name still fits — it captures both under- and over-population implicitly via the survival rule. ✅
- **`nextGeneration(cells)`**: Computes the next board state. Clear. ✅

**Decision**: Keep all names. They accurately reveal intent. No new functionality from test 5 changed the nature of any function — overpopulation was already subsumed by `survives`.

### Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

```
isNeighbor (lines 3-7):
- Constants: 0, 0, 1, 1 (literals in dx!==0, dy!==0, dx<=1, dy<=1) = 4
- Bindings: a, b, dx, dy = 4
- Invocations: Math.abs ×2, subtraction ×2 = 4 calls → 8
- Conditionals: the boolean expression (||, &&, comparisons) — 1 logical combination ≈ 4
- Assignments: dx=, dy= (const bindings, count as bindings not mutations) → already in bindings
Subtotal ≈ 4 + 4 + 8 + 4 = 20

countLiveNeighbors (lines 9-10):
- Bindings: cell, cells, other = 3
- Invocations: filter, isNeighbor, .length = ~3 → 6
- Loops: filter = 5
Subtotal ≈ 3 + 6 + 5 = 14

survives (lines 12-13):
- Constants: 2, 3 = 2
- Bindings: liveNeighbors = 1
- Conditionals: === ×2 with || ≈ 4
Subtotal ≈ 2 + 1 + 4 = 7

nextGeneration (lines 15-16):
- Bindings: cells, cell = 2
- Invocations: filter, survives, countLiveNeighbors = 3 → 6
- Loops: filter = 5
Subtotal ≈ 2 + 6 + 5 = 13

Total Mass ≈ 54
```

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules (priority order)

**Rule 1 — Tests Pass**: ✅ All 5 pass.

**Rule 2 — Reveals Intent**: Code is already highly expressive. Each function is a single-responsibility, well-named pure function. The domain reads naturally: `nextGeneration` keeps cells that `survive` based on `countLiveNeighbors`. No improvement available without harming clarity.

**Rule 3 — No Duplication**: No duplicated logic. `isNeighbor` is the single source of adjacency knowledge; `survives` is the single source of the survival rule. The `0 || ... && ...` in `isNeighbor` is one expression, not duplication.

**Rule 4 — Fewest Elements**: The four small functions are all necessary and each carries distinct domain meaning. Inlining any of them would lower the element count but destroy intent-revealing structure — Rule 2 trumps Rule 4. No over-engineering present (no unused abstractions, no premature generality).

### Step 4 & 5: Refactoring Attempt and Mass Comparison

I evaluated one concrete candidate: inlining `dx`/`dy` in `isNeighbor` or extracting an explaining variable. Inlining would slightly reduce bindings but make the expression harder to read (Rule 2 loss). Extracting more would add elements (Rule 4 loss) with no clarity gain. Both rejected.

I also considered extracting the magic numbers in `survives` (2, 3) into named constants. However, in the Game of Life domain these numbers are canonical and the function name `survives` already provides the semantic context; naming them would add elements (Rule 4) without revealing more intent than `survives` already does. Rejected.

### Step 6: Decision — No Refactoring Warranted

```
Refactoring Evaluation:
- ❌ Naming: All four names (isNeighbor, countLiveNeighbors, survives,
     nextGeneration) already clearly reveal intent. Test 5 added no new
     behavior — overpopulation was already subsumed by `survives`, so no
     name's meaning shifted.
- ❌ Duplication (Rule 3): No obvious or conceptual duplication. Adjacency
     and survival rules each have a single representation.
- ❌ Fewest Elements (Rule 4): Each function carries distinct domain
     meaning. Inlining to reduce mass would sacrifice clarity (Rule 2
     trumps APP and Rule 4).
- ❌ Mass (APP ≈ 54): Distributed across four minimal pure functions;
     attempts to lower it (inlining dx/dy, removing helpers) all cost
     clarity for no real gain.

Reasoning:
The implementation is already clean and at a natural optimum for this
stage. The most recent Green phase made no implementation change — the
overpopulation rule fell out of the existing `survives` predicate, which
is strong evidence the design is well-factored. Every candidate
refactoring I evaluated traded away Rule 2 (clarity) or added Rule 4
elements for at best a marginal mass reduction. Per the priority order,
clarity wins, so no refactoring is performed.
```

### Step 7: Completion

Confirming tests remain green (no code was changed):

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Refactoring**: None performed — documented why (see below).
**Mass Change**: Unchanged (APP ≈ 54; no candidate refactoring improved it without sacrificing clarity).
**Tests**: All 5 passing, 2 todo. ✅

**Summary of evaluation** (priority order applied):
- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All green before and after.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: `isNeighbor`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survives`, `nextGeneration` all clearly name their domain responsibilities. Test 5 (overpopulation) added no new behavior — it fell out of the existing `survives` predicate — so no function's name or nature shifted.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: Adjacency and survival rules each have a single source of truth.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Four minimal pure functions, each with distinct meaning. Inlining helpers or extracting `dx`/`dy` would lower element count/mass but cost clarity — Rule 2 trumps APP and Rule 4, so rejected.

The fact that the overpopulation test required zero implementation change is strong evidence the design is already well-factored. No refactoring is warranted.
